entry.title: Re: Impacts of Business Rules modifying Effective Start D...
entry.content:
@muralidharansrinivasan within the comments it seems a solution has been provided.

the final comment was:

Hi @muralidharansrinivasan my previous comment wasn't clear—the date (past or future) doesn't matter. If pre-existing data corruption issues are present, they can still result in these errors and must be cleaned manually. This should be rare, and once cleaned, the issue should not recur. No patch can fix existing corrupted data; customers experiencing this must raise a support ticket for manual intervention.

Please accept this as a solution in case all your issues are fixed now.

entry.title: Re: AI and Joule Upskilling Guidance for SAP Functional C...
entry.content:
Hello!

First start with foundation AI and ML fundamentals, SAP's Business AI strategy, and a broad overview of SAP Joule. The free SAP Business AI Fundamentals digital badge course on learning.sap.com is the ideal entry point.

Next, focus on SAP Platform: move into SAP AI Core, AI Launchpad, the Generative AI Hub, and BTP integration. This is where you learn how SAP's AI infrastructure actually works,  model deployment, orchestration, and lifecycle management.

And lastly  focus on Agentic AI, RAG pipelines, prompt engineering, AI evaluation, and delivering real use cases. By this stage you should be building and testing on the BTP 30-day free trial environment.

 

SAP Learning Resources provided below:

1. Generative AI: LLMs, Embeddings & Vector Databases

Key concepts to learn: LLM architecture (transformer models), tokens, context windows, embeddings as numerical vector representations of text, vector databases for semantic search and retrieval, RAG pipelines, and the SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine.

https://learning.sap.com/courses/discovering-sap-s-generative-ai-hub

https://learning.sap.com/courses/using-advance-ai-techniques-with-sap-s-generative-ai-hub?searchId=1ad1e465-eb20-4de9-8b70-9ddaa247bd7d&listPosition=1

https://learning.sap.com/courses/solve-your-business-problems-using-prompts-and-llms-in-sap-generative-ai-hub?searchId=7cf8865d-c1c7-47c3-97e9-1cb8e3c6283e&listPosition=1

https://architecture.learning.sap.com/docs/ref-arch/e5eb3b9b1d

https://architecture.learning.sap.com/docs/ref-arch/e5eb3b9b1d

 

2. Agentic AI: Multi-Agent Systems, Planning & Reasoning

Key concepts to learn: The difference between Generative AI and Agentic AI (autonomy, planning, tool use), multi-agent orchestration and collaboration patterns, LangChain and LangGraph frameworks for agent workflows, the Joule Agent Discovery Workshop methodology, and identifying high-value agentic use cases across business processes.

https://learning.sap.com/courses/discovering-high-value-opportunities-for-agentic-ai?searchId=e5cd5846-bf67-4478-8e9e-3948731cb07a&listPosition=1

 

3. AI Lifecycle Management

Key concepts to learn: Model deployment strategies (auto vs. manual upgrade), resource group management in SAP AI Core, orchestration service configuration, model monitoring and inference observability, compliance and governance (GDPR, ISO 27001, SOC 1/SOC 2), and SAP Cloud ALM for application lifecycle management.

https://learning.sap.com/courses/discovering-sap-s-generative-ai-hub?searchId=22e443a6-2f8f-4692-af1d-4302650a65a1&listPosition=1

https://learning.sap.com/courses/introduction-to-ai-core?searchId=8e64c26e-2544-49a8-b68d-bb29a161491c&listPosition=1

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-architecture-blog-posts/discovery-first-ai-assessment-for-sap-landscapes-an-essay/ba-p/14297147

 

4. Machine Learning Algorithms

Key concepts to learn:  Supervised Learning: Classification (invoice fraud detection, customer churn), regression (demand forecasting, price optimization), decision trees, random forests, and gradient boosting.

Key concepts to learn: Unsupervised Learning: Clustering (customer segmentation, anomaly detection), dimensionality reduction (PCA), and association rules for patterns like market basket analysis.

Search "SAP Analytics Cloud predictive" on learning.sap.com.

 

5. Neural Networks

Key concepts to learn: Perceptrons and activation functions, forward and back propagation, deep learning architectures (CNNs, RNNs, LSTMs), transformer architecture with attention mechanisms, BERT and GPT model families, transfer learning, and the risks associated with neural networks including hallucinations, bias, and data privacy.

https://www.udemy.com/course/sap-ai-masterclass/?srsltid=AfmBOord2tspN_7bVn3g6_d35wu1acVov3LeTRjLmz4CW1KO8KuwdPyJ

 

6. Natural Language Processing (NLP)

Key concepts to learn: Tokenization, stemming, lemmatization, word embeddings (Word2Vec, sentence transformers), TF-IDF and cosine similarity for document retrieval, Named Entity Recognition (NER), intent classification, sentiment analysis, text summarization, and the SAP Translation Hub and Document Information Extraction services.

Search each service name in https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/

 

7. Prompt Engineering

Key concepts to learn: Zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought prompting, prompt templates with input variables and system vs. user role separation, grounding prompts with business context (RAG integration), content filtering and data masking in prompt design, prompt versioning and registry management in SAP AI Launchpad, and avoiding hallucinations through deliberate prompt structure.

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-btp-ai-best-practices-a-deep-dive-into-key-technical-foundations/ba-p/14221812

 

8. AI Evaluation Frameworks

Key concepts to learn: Evaluation metrics including accuracy, BLEU, ROUGE, and RAGAS (for RAG pipelines), hallucination detection and mitigation, content filtering and output safety validation, bias and fairness assessment in enterprise AI, A/B testing AI models in production, and AI audit logging and compliance reporting under GDPR and

The SAP AI Launchpad documentation on help.sap.com covers monitoring, observability, and audit logging in detail: https://help.sap.com/docs/ai-launchpad?locale=en-US

 

9. SAP Joule: Functional Consultant Focus

SAP Joule for Consultants is purpose-built as an AI copilot that delivers concise answers, explains custom code, and provides expert-level best practice guidance, grounded in SAP's proprietary knowledge base so consultants can rely on the information to be reliable, responsible, and relevant.

https://learning.sap.com/courses/introducing-sap-joule-for-consultants

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-joule-for-consultants-listening-learning-and-delivering/ba-p/14236226

 

Public Cloud vs. Private Cloud

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: Joule is natively embedded and available out-of-the-box. The SAP Generative AI Hub is accessible via standard BTP subscription. Pre-built AI services such as Document Extraction and Translation Hub are available immediately without additional provisioning. AI capabilities are updated automatically with each quarterly release. The SAP Discovery Center (www.discovery-center.cloud.sap ) is the recommended starting point for finding BTP Generative AI missions and reference architectures.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Require explicit BTP AI Foundation provisioning and configuration. The advantage is greater flexibility customers can deploy custom AI models via AI Core, build bespoke agentic workflows via Joule Studio, and integrate their own LLMs. RISE with SAP customers should confirm AI Foundation entitlements with their SAP account team before beginning configuration.

The key practical difference for functional consultants: in Public Cloud you start using Joule immediately and extend it through standard configuration; in Private Cloud you have a setup phase involving BTP AI Foundation provisioning before Joule and the Generative AI Hub become available, but you gain more control over what models are used and how data flows.

 

Certifications and additional reference

SAP Generative AI Developer Certification (C_AIG): https://learning.sap.com/certifications/sap-certified-associate-sap-generative-ai-developer?gad_source=1&gad_campaignid=22927669587&gbraid=0AAAABAv-ik-IdNYynF6lexIzb5065abOe&gclid=Cj0KCQjwlqTRBhCBARIsANrkrxiyF2QsserNYTebOcsb3z045n7YBNP3gcwt9OgO9IE5KWNYAqTFMRAaAlM7EALw_wcB

SAP Business AI Fundamentals (free digital badge): This is the recommended first step. It covers SAP's Business AI strategy, principles, ethics, privacy standards, and business opportunities, and results in a digital badge as a Record of Achievement. Find it by searching "SAP Business AI" on learning.sap.com.

 

Official SAP platforms:

SAP Learning (learning.sap.com) for all official courses, learning journeys, and certifications.

SAP Discovery Center (discovery-center.cloud.sap) for BTP missions and reference architectures.

SAP Community (community.sap.com) for blogs, Q&A, and expert content.

SAP Architecture Center (architecture.learning.sap.com) for BTP AI reference architectures.

SAP Help Portal (help.sap.com) for product documentation, including AI Launchpad lifecycle management docs at help.sap.com/docs/ai-launchpad.

entry.title: IAS Configuration related queries
entry.content:
Hi team,

Hope you are doing well!

We need your help to understand the below points regarding IAS/IPS configurations

1) We need to configure email templates for onboarding in 7+ languages. We understand that the templates must be created using both plain text and HTML formats. Do we need to manually create these templates in both formats for all languages, or is there any alternative approach available?

2) We understand that Locale and Country data can be synced from SuccessFactors to IAS. However, for email template creation, we need to trigger emails based on country-specific conditions. For example, if the country is the Netherlands, the email language should be Dutch, and if the country is Malaysia, the email language should be Malay. Is this achievable by configuring multiple languages within the same template set? Currently, in onboarding, the locale for all users is defaulted to en_US. We need to assign the locale within the IAS system based on the country. Could you please assist with this?

3) We would like to sync a custom HRIS field (Employee Group) from Job Information (Malaysia) in SuccessFactors to the IAS system to filter email templates. Could you please confirm whether it is possible to sync a custom field to IAS? If yes, kindly share any relevant KBA or documentation on how to achieve this.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Intermittent 500/XHR Errors While Accessing Custom Node.js On-Prem Service via SAP BTP Cloud Connect
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am facing an intermittent issue while accessing a custom on-premise Node.js service through SAP BTP Connectivity and SAP Cloud Connector. My setup consists of a React frontend running in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), a Node.js backend deployed in BTP, and an on-premise Node.js server exposed through Cloud Connector using a destination with ProxyType=OnPremise. The architecture is basically: React Frontend → BTP Node.js Backend → Connectivity Service → Cloud Connector → On-Prem Node.js Server.

 

 

 

The strange part is that the issue only occurs with my custom Node.js service and not with SAP systems connected through the same Cloud Connector and destination setup. From the BTP backend, I am able to access the on-prem endpoint consistently without any issue using axios calls. However, when the frontend invokes the backend API, the request intermittently fails with XHR 500 errors or messages like “Access denied to resource … ensure to expose the resource correctly in your cloud connector.” Even more confusing is that the failure happens even when the on-prem endpoint only returns a very small plain-text response (~2 KB). So this does not appear to be related to payload size or backend load.

 

 

I have also recently configured a Cloud Connector Location ID and added the same Location ID in the BTP destination configuration. Has anyone faced similar intermittent issues specifically with non-SAP/custom Node.js servers while SAP systems continue to work normally through the same Cloud Connector setup?

Any guidance or troubleshooting suggestions would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: 回复： Custom App Interface Publishing Issues of ABAP Development Tools platform
entry.content:
Subject: How to publish dual-scenario OData V4 services (Fiori App with Draft vs. Web API without Draft) on the same base table in ADT?

Body: I am developing a custom application in the ABAP Development Tools (ADT) platform using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP). I need to support two different scenarios simultaneously based on the same underlying database table (TAB1). These two scenarios must be published separately and must not interfere with each other.

• Scenario 1 (UI Fiori App): Published as an OData V4 - UI service for front-end users. It requires field-level controls (such as read-only and mandatory fields) and must support Draft Mode.

• Scenario 2 (Web API): Published as an OData V4 - Web API service for external system integration. It must support standard CRUD operations (Create, Update, and Query by conditions) and should ideally write directly to the database, bypassing Draft Mode.

Current Dilemma:

• If I configure the Behavior Definition (BDEF) without draft mode to accommodate the Web API, the Fiori App functionality is compromised because I cannot implement the required UI field controls (like read-only) properly within the BDEF context.

• If I configure the BDEF with draft mode to satisfy the Fiori App, the external Web API calls cannot directly update or query the active database tables without dealing with draft administrative data. How can I design the architecture to fully compatible both scenarios without them affecting each other?

entry.title: Bare Minimum End-to-End Master Data Setup for a Successful MRP Run in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
1. Introduction

Material Requirements Planning (MRP) is the backbone of production planning in SAP S/4HANA. A successful MRP run depends on having the correct master data in place.

This blog provides a practical, no-nonsense guide to the absolute minimum master data setup required to execute a successful multi-level MRP run.

The scenario: Raw Materials → Semi-Finished Good → Finished Good, planned via MRP with results visible in MD04.

2. Master Data Flow & Dependencies

Before you start, understand the sequence. Each step depends on the previous one:

Tip (S/4HANA Public Cloud): The transaction codes listed throughout this blog (e.g., MM01, CS01, CR01, CA01, C223) are not accessible via SAP GUI in S/4HANA Public Cloud. You can find the corresponding Fiori apps by searching for the transaction code directly in the Fiori Launchpad search bar. For example, searching MM01 returns the Manage Materials app.

Important: Copying a material in MM01 does NOT copy its BOM, Routing, or Production Version. You must create these separately for each material.

3. Step 1: Material Master (MM01)

Transaction: MM01 (Create) | MM02 (Change)

You need three types of materials. Select the following views when creating:

  • Raw Material: Basic Data + MRP Data
  • Semi-Finished: Basic Data + MRP Data + Work Scheduling
  • Finished Good: Basic Data + MRP Data + Work Scheduling

Tip: MM01 is technically 'create material views'. If you forgot to create a view, go back to MM01 (not MM02) and select the missing view - it will create it for the existing material.

3.1 Minimum Fields

Basic Data

Field

Value

Base Unit of Measure

PC (Pieces) - or appropriate UoM

Material Group

Your Material Group (e.g., L002 - Raw Materials, L004 - Finished Goods, L003 - Semi Finished)

MRP Header Fields

Field

Value

Plant

Your Plant (e.g. 1010)

Storage Location

Your storage location ( e.g. 101A - Finished Goods, 101B - Semi Finished, 101C - Raw Material )

 MRP 1

Field

Value

Why

Base Unit of Measure

PC (Pieces) - or appropriate UoM

 

MRP Type

PD

Enables MRP planning for this material

Lot Sizing Procedure

EX (Exact)

Lot-for-lot planning

MRP Controller

Your MRP Controller (e.g., 001)

Determines the person or team responsible for the availability of the material.

Purchasing Group

Your Purchasing Group (for e.g., 001)

Represents a specific buyer or a group of buyers responsible for procuring the material.

MRP 2

Field

Value

Why

Procurement Type

F (External)
E (In-House Production)
X (Both Procurement Types)

Raw materials are always purchased, never produced. Hence, use F (External) for Raw materials.

Planned Delivery Time

1 (day)

Rough lead time for procurement

Production Storage Location

Your storage location

Where procured material is stored

In-house production time

1 (day)

Approximate time in days that is required for a material that is to be produced in-house. Valid for Semi-Finished and Finished Goods Materials.

MRP 3

Field

Value

Why

Period Indicator

M (Months)

Planning period granularity

Strategy Group

10 (Make-to-Stock)

Production goes into shared stock

MRP 4

Nothing to maintain. Just navigate through the tab so it gets created.

3.2 Semi-Finished Good - Minimum Fields

Same as Raw Material PLUS the following differences:

MRP 2 - Key Difference

Field

Value

Why

Procurement Type

E (In-house production)

We produce this material ourselves

In-house Production Time

1 (day)

Rough production lead time (independent of quantity)

Planned Delivery Time

1 (day)

Overall time to procure/produce

Production Storage Location

Your storage location

Where produced material goes

Work Scheduling

Must be created (navigate to the tab) but no fields are mandatory to fill. Without this view, you cannot use production orders.

Note on In-house Production Time: This is only for MRP rough planning. It does NOT determine actual operation durations - that comes from the Routing. It's also independent of quantity (whether you produce 1 or 100, MRP uses the same rough lead time).

3.3 Finished Good - Minimum Fields

Identical to Semi-Finished Good. Same views, same fields, same values. Procurement Type = E (In-house production).

4. Step 2: Bill of Materials (CS01)

Transaction: CS01 (Create) | CS02 (Change) | CS03 (Display)

The BOM lists which components are needed to produce one unit of your Semi-Finished and Finished Goods material. Create a BOM for each Semi-Finished and Finished Good.

4.1 Header Fields

Field

Value

Material

Your semi-finished or Finished Good

Plant

Your plant (e.g., 1010)

BOM Usage

1 (Production)

4.2 Item List - Minimum Fields

Field

Value

Component

Material number of the component

Quantity

How many needed per 1 unit of header material

UoM

PC (Pieces)

Item Category

L (Stock item)

To produce 1 Finished Goods material ZKU_FG_N1, you need 1 PC of Semi-Finished material ZKU_SEMI_N1 and Semi-Finished material ZKU_SEMI_N2.

To produce 1 Semi-Finished material ZKU_SEMI_N1, you need 1 PC of Raw Material ZKU_RAW_N1.

To produce 1 Semi-Finished material ZKU_SEMI_N2, you need 1 PC of Raw Material ZKU_RAW_N2. (Screenshot not shown)

5. Step 3: Work Center (CR01)

Transaction: CR01 (Create) | CR02 (Change) | CR03 (Display)

The Work Center defines WHERE production happens and determines operation durations via scheduling formulas.

Tip: If a work center already exists, copy it when creating a new one - it saves significant effort.

5.1 Basic Data

Field

Value

Work Center Category

Machine (0001)

Standard Value Key

SAP1 - defines parameters: Setup, Machine, Labor

5.2 Capacities - Key Fields

Field

Value

Notes

Factory Calendar

Your factory calendar

Determines working days

Start Time

07:00

When work center starts operating

Finish Time

16:00

When work center stops operating

Capacity Utilization %

100

Percentage of time that is productive

No. of Individual Capacities

1

How many parallel machines

Relevant to Scheduling

Checked

Must be checked for scheduling to work


Click on the Capacity button.

5.3 Scheduling Formulas

The formulas determine how operation duration is calculated from the standard values in the Routing:

  • Setup Duration = Setup Time (from routing)
  • Processing Duration = Machine Time × Quantity

These are typically pre-configured (SAP006 or similar). Just ensure they exist - they are maintained on the Scheduling tab.

Click on the Intervals button.

Go back and click on Intervals and Shifts.

6. Step 4: Routing (CA01)

Transaction: CA01 (Create) | CA02 (Change) | CA03 (Display)

The Routing defines HOW production happens - which steps, on which work centers, and how long each step takes.

6.1 Header - Minimum Fields

Field

Value

Notes

Usage

1 (Production)

 

Overall Status

4 (Released)

MUST be 4, otherwise MRP won't use it

Lot Size From

1

 

Lot Size To

99999999

Maximum possible lot size

Click on the Operations button.

6.2 Operations - Minimum Fields

Field

Value

Notes

Work Center

Your work center

Where this operation runs

Control Key

PP01 or YBP1

Depends on system customizing; must enable scheduling

Machine Time

e.g., 10 MIN

Time per piece (multiplied by quantity)

Labor Time

Same as Machine Time

Set equal to machine time for simplicity

How duration is calculated: Setup Time + (Machine Time × Quantity). Example: If Machine Time = 10 min and you produce 6 pieces, the operation takes 60 minutes.

Tip: Double-click the operation number to open details view - it's much easier to maintain standard values there than in the table. Avoid multiple scheduled operations on the same work center. The scheduling APIs don't handle this well. If you need multiple steps, use different work centers.

7. Step 5: Production Version (C223)

Transaction: C223

The Production Version is the LINK between your material, its BOM, and its Routing. Without it, MRP doesn't know how to produce the material.

7.1 Minimum Fields

Field

Value

Notes

Production Version Name

e.g., DEF1

Per material; system takes alphabetically first by default

Lock

Not locked

 

Lot Size Min

1

Must match routing lot sizes

Lot Size Max

99999999

Must match routing lot sizes

Valid From

Today's date

 

Valid To

31.12.9999

Latest possible date

Task List Type

Routing

Select via F4 help → search by material

Task List Group/Counter

Use F4 help

Search by material to find the routing

BOM Alternative

Use F4 help

Select the BOM you created

Production Line

Your main work center

Required by SAP Cloud apps even for discrete manufacturing

7.2 Verification

After filling all fields, click 'Check'. You should see:

  • Green traffic light for Task List - Routing exists and is valid
  • Green traffic light for BOM - BOM exists and is valid

Then: Save Changes & Close Screen → Save in the main transaction.

Important: Lot sizes in the Production Version MUST match the lot sizes in the Routing. If they don't match, the system may fail to find the connection.

8. Step 6: Creating Demand

Transaction: VA01 (Create Sales Order)

Before running MRP, you need to create demand for your Finished Goods Material. There are multiple ways to create demand, for example: Planned Independent Requirements (PIRs), Sales Order, or by simply creating a planned order.  In this blog we are using Sales Order to create demand.

9. Step 7: Running MRP

App: Schedule MRP Runs (Fiori) or Background Job with template 'Requirements Planning MRP'

In S/4HANA Cloud, the classic MD01/MD02 transactions may not be available. Instead, use the 'Schedule MRP Runs' Fiori app to create a background job.

 

9.1 MRP Run Parameters

Parameter

Value

Notes

Job Template

Requirements Planning MRP

 

Recurrence

Single Run / Start Immediately

 

Material

Your finished good

Multi-level run will plan lower levels too

Plant

Your plant

 

Scheduling

2 (Lead time + Capacity scheduling)

CRITICAL: Without this, planned orders have no scheduling tab and no capacity requirements

Planning Mode

3 (Delete and recreate)

Clean slate for testing; use other modes in production

10. Step 8: Evaluating Results in MD04

Transaction: MD04 (Stock/Requirements List)

MD04 is your primary tool to verify MRP results. Enter a material and plant to see its complete supply/demand picture.

The MRP run will create demand (sales order) and supply elements (planned orders and purchase requisitions) in the system to fulfil the demand created for your finished goods material.

11. Conclusion

With the above bare minimum steps, you can achieve a complete multi-level MRP run in SAP S/4HANA. Start simple with one finished good, one semi-finished, and two raw materials. Once this works, expand to more complex multi-level trees.

entry.title: Re: CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite / The Download → Git → Deploy pipeline
entry.content:
@RubikWal Thanks for the question — and you are right that transports preserve externalized parameters after the first import and handle a clean re-import without manual cleanup. For teams whose needs end at "move a package from DEV to TST to PRD," transports are a perfectly good answer.

The reason we still built this comes down to a few gaps transports don't cover:

  • Deletions and package moves propagate automatically (no manual cleanup, no unique-key exceptions on internal IDs).
  • Activation of IFlows, mappings, script collections, and value mappings is handled from the Git delta — no Excel tracking after a bulk transport import.
  • Git as the source of truth — every change is diffable and auditable, and a new tenant (POC, scale-out, DR) can be set up from scratch directly from Git.

To be clear: CTS+ and CTMS are the official SAP offerings for transport, with the limitations above. This here is just an open-source option for teams who want Git-based governance and end-to-end automation. Running both in parallel is fine during a cutover phase, but as a permanent setup I wouldn't recommend it.

entry.title: Re: Request for BAPI to Enhance Inbound Delivery Validation
entry.content:
Good day @sakar_korde94c0f8 

Thank you for your question.

For Public Cloud, you should not plan this as a classic BAPI enhancement. BAPIs are not the recommended extensibility approach in Public Cloud. You need to check whether there is a released API or released BAdI for the exact inbound delivery process.

For your specific requirement, the duplicate check is linked to the inbound delivery External ID/ASN reference. Note that the ASN field in cloud warehouse management is a reference number for the External ID from the LE inbound delivery, and that it is not independently editable in the warehouse app. See KBA 3669017 for more insights.

What that means is:

  • Do not use a classic BAPI for this in Public Edition.
  • If the inbound delivery is created by interface, validate the vendor invoice number before or during the inbound API call.
  • If the field is entered manually in a standard inbound delivery app, check whether a released delivery validation BAdI exists for the exact app and process.
  • A CDS view can help you find existing External ID values, but it cannot block saving by itself.
  • If no released BAdI exists for inbound delivery save validation, then blocking the duplicate directly in the standard app is not supported.

 

Here is the recommended approach:

  1. Check the source of the inbound delivery (Interface/API creation, Manual creation, EWM inbound delivery replication).
  2. If it was created by interface, perform the duplicate check in the middleware or calling system before creating the inbound delivery.
  3. Use released inbound delivery APIs only where available in Business Accelerator Hub.
  4. For reporting or validation lookup, check released CDS views such as I_InboundDelivery, I_InboundDeliveryItem and relevant warehouse inbound delivery header views in View Browser.                               
  5. If you require manual save validation, open Custom logic and search for released BAdIs under Delivery Management. Note that the BAdI must match the inbound delivery creation or change process you are using.           

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/aff0b3f5f46c42a2b2c0fabfc233bab2/78ac6e6632494a8dbc0a63c8b63f6d77.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Facing this error while processing "HU is not in...
entry.content:
Hello @Balaji1507 

Thank you for your question.

That error you described means the HU exists, but it is not located in the storage bin assigned to the packing work center you selected. In Packing Handling Units Advanced, the HU must be in the selected work center before you can repack it.

More details is provided here: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/43d76f702805455f98df936e64ce6a97.html?locale=en-US

What that means in simple terms is:

  • The fact that you created an HU does not automatically mean it is available for repacking.
  • The HU must be in the same warehouse and storage bin assigned to the work center.
  • IF the HU is in another bin, another work center, or only created without being moved into the packing bin, the system blocks changes.

Check the following configuration in your system:

In Configure Work Center app, check the relevant warehouse number, confirm that the packing work center exists, then confirm the assigned storage bin for the work center.                                                            

Check that the bin used in Pack Handling Units Advanced is the same bin assigned to the work center. Make sure the storage type and bin allow packing work center processing. Check that the HU is not locked by another process or open warehouse task.

Here is a quick check:

If the HU is in Warehouse Monitor but its bin is different from the work center bin, then the configuration is probably fine. You only need to move the HU to the correct work center bin before repacking.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Simplify the downtime-optimized System Conversion to SAP S/4HANA with Uptime Customizing for FI
entry.content:
We are planning an ECC 6.0 EHP7 system conversion (Oracle DB, >20 TB, hosted on AWS) to SAP S/4HANA using SUM with DMO in a downtime-optimized conversion (doC) approach, potentially leveraging DMOVE2S4.

As per SAP Note 3444013 – Executing and Monitoring Downtime-Optimized Conversion to SAP S/4HANA with Uptime Customizing for Finance Conversion, starting from SUM 2.0 SP25, a prior standard conversion run is no longer mandatory for FIN customizing. The note indicates that FIN customizing can either be taken from transports of a previous run or created directly on the temporary system during the doC run. 

However, in the SAP Learning course on downtime-optimized conversion (Minimizing downtime of System Conversion to SAP S/4HANA with down), it is mentioned that a standard conversion run is first required to generate and include the FIN customizing transport requests in the customer transport buffer.

Could you please clarify the following:

  1. With SUM 2.0 SP25 and the Uptime Customizing for Finance Conversion feature, is it fully supported to skip the standard conversion run in Sandbox specifically for FIN customizing preparation?
  2. Is it recommended and practically feasible to perform all required FIN uptime customizing directly during the doC run (temporary instance) instead of relying on transports from a prior standard conversion?
  3. Are there any limitations, risks, or best practice considerations when adopting this approach (especially for large systems >20 TB)?

entry.title: Re: Hierachy BoM with Supplier name for part
entry.content: As far as I know, CS12 does not provide supplier information for BOM components in standard SAP, as BOM data does not contain vendor information. If required, vendor data may be derived from purchasing objects such as Purchasing Info Records or Source Lists, depending on the customer's sourcing setup. Therefore, this information is not available as a standard field in CS12.

entry.title: Re: Why only China & standard chart of accounts, when my...
entry.content:
Hi @JUNJUNPAN , 

When you activate company codes in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, the system does not automatically create a separate Chart of Accounts (CoA) for every country.

In the SAP S/4HANA Cloud standard content, YCOA is the operative Chart of Accounts delivered by SAP and assigned to all company codes by default. It contains the G/L accounts used for master data, account determination, and SAP Best Practices processes.

Some countries have an additional pre-delivered local (alternative) Chart of Accounts. However, these are only available for specific countries/regions defined by SAP.

In your case:

  • China has a pre-delivered local CoA:
    • Country: China (CN)
    • Alternative CoA: YACN – China Chart of Accounts
  • Hong Kong and USA do not have a pre-delivered alternative/local CoA in the SAP standard content. Therefore, activating those company codes will not automatically create additional charts of accounts for them.

A list of the alternative (local) chart of accounts specific to a particular country/region is included in the documentation about the standard chart of accounts YCOA. That documentation is available here: https://help.sap.com/docs/s4hana-best-practices/s4cld-coa.

This documentation also includes an FAQ topic with questions specific to the predelivered G/L accounts and account determination.

So, there is no missing activation step on your side. The behavior is standard SAP functionality.

If a predelivered alternative COA isn't available, you can create your own in your configuration environment, starting with the Edit Chart of Accounts List configuration activity. For more information, see

You can find more details in the SAP documentation:  How to Create an Alternative Chart of Accounts.

Additional information SAP note 3405359: https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3405359

Best regards, Elena P.

entry.title: Re: Why only China & standard chart of accounts, when my...
entry.content:
Good day @JUNJUNPAN 

Thank you for your question.

What you describe is not uncommon in Public Cloud. Activating a company code for a country or region does not always create a separate local chart of accounts. YCOA is the standard operating chart of accounts used by company codes. A separate local or alternative chart of accounts appears only if SAP delivers one for that country or if you created one where it is allowed.

China my have delivered local chart/account content, which is why you see it. Hong Kong and the USA may not automatically create a separate chart of accounts, this does not mean the company code activation was incomplete.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/cb9b75f01584475fa04952c11a67f9fc.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/37de4a1a09b54b689073d8e4f57527f1.html?locale=en-US

I recommend you check the following:

  • In Manage Your Solution or CBC, check the activated country version for China, Hong Kong and USA.        
  • Open Manage G/L Account Master Data, filter by operating chart of accounts YCOA. Check if company codes exist for the China, Hong Kong, and USA company codes.                                                 

You can check KBA 3405359 if local reporting requires an alternative COA. If no local COA is delivered and the business needs one, evaluate creating an alternative chart of accounts through the supported configuration approach. See KBA 3426847 for more details.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

Trying to build it I received the following:

1> database_sqlany.obj : error LNK2019: unresolved external symbol "int __cdecl sqlany_initialize_interface(struct SQLAnywhereInterface *,char const *)" (?sqlany_initialize_interface@@YAHPAUSQLAnywhereInterface@@PBD@Z) referenced in function "public: __thiscall SQLAnyDatabase::SQLAnyDatabase(int,class std::basic_string<wchar_t,struct std::char_traits<wchar_t>,class std::allocator<wchar_t> > const &)" (??0SQLAnyDatabase@@QAE@HABV?$basic_string@_WU?$char_traits@_W@std@@V?$allocator@_W@2@@std@@@Z)
1> database_sqlany.obj : error LNK2019: unresolved external symbol "void __cdecl sqlany_finalize_interface(struct SQLAnywhereInterface *)" (?sqlany_finalize_interface@@YAXPAUSQLAnywhereInterface@@@Z) referenced in function "public: __thiscall SQLAnyDatabase::SQLAnyDatabase(int,class std::basic_string<wchar_t,struct std::char_traits<wchar_t>,class std::allocator<wchar_t> > const &)" (??0SQLAnyDatabase@@QAE@HABV?$basic_string@_WU?$char_traits@_W@std@@V?$allocator@_W@2@@std@@@Z)
1> ..\dbhandler\vc_mswuddll\sqlany.dll : fatal error LNK1120: 2 unresolved externals

 

What am I missing?

 

Thank you,

P.S.:

In my code I follow the sample code which have:

[code]

if( !sqlany_initialize_interface( &api, NULL ) ) {
printf( "Could not initialize the interface!\n" );
exit( 0 );
}

if( !api.sqlany_init( "MyAPP", SQLANY_API_VERSION_1, &max_api_ver )) {
printf( "Failed to initialize the interface! Supported version = %d\n", max_api_ver );
sqlany_finalize_interface( &api );
return -1;
}

/* A connection object needs to be created first */
sqlany_conn = api.sqlany_new_connection();

[/code]

entry.title: Re: Not able to filter out data by Date wise at header le...
entry.content:
Hello @AJAYKUMAR1 !

The "Created On" field is currently available only at the Line Item level in the standard Manage Billing Documents (F0797) application and therefore cannot be used as a Header-level filter in the standard app behavior.

App documentation here: 

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/7b24a64d9d0941bda1afa753263d9e39/d2185b549a8c8e4ce10000000a4450e5.html


SAP provides extensibility capabilities for billing applications through Custom Fields and Logic and UI adaptation features. However, exposing additional standard fields as Header filter criteria depends on whether the application supports this extension scenario.

Therefore, the following options can be considered:

  • verify whether the field can be enabled through available UI adaptation/extensibility options;
  • evaluate creation of a custom app/report/CDS-based solution if Header-level filtering is a business requirement;

See more about App Extensibility: Manage Billing Documents here https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/7b24a64d9d0941bda1afa753263d9e39/30f3db567e662e7be10000000a44147b.html

Regards, Elena P.

entry.title: Calling the SAP Business Partner API from Kyma without Serverless
entry.content:
Introduction

My previous post walked through calling the SAP Business Partner API from a Kyma Serverless Function. This post covers what happens when that approach hits real-world constraints — and how to adapt.

The two problems I encountered on a managed Kyma cluster:

  1. The Serverless module wasn't installed — kind: Function doesn't exist as a resource type, so the function YAML simply won't apply
  2. No permission to create BTP Destinations via the cockpit — the New Destination button was greyed out

Both are common in corporate and training environments where you don't own the cluster or subaccount. Here's how to work around both.

By the end you'll have the same result as the original post — a live endpoint returning SAP Business Partner JSON — but deployed as a standard Kubernetes Deployment instead of a Kyma Function.

What Changed vs the Original Approach

Original This Post

kind: Function (Kyma Serverless)kind: Deployment (standard Kubernetes)
Code inline in YAMLCode in a Docker container
No registry neededImage pushed to GitHub Container Registry
Destination created in BTP cockpitDestination created via Destination Service REST API

The call chain is identical — your container still calls XSUAA for a token, resolves the destination, then calls the SAP API sandbox. The difference is purely in how the code is packaged and deployed.

Prerequisites

  • kubectl configured against a Kyma cluster
  • Docker installed locally
  • A GitHub account (for the container registry)
  • An account on api.sap.com with an API key

Step 1: Check What's Actually Available

Before spending time on a Kyma Function approach, verify whether Serverless is installed:

kubectl api-resources | grep serverless

If nothing is returned, the Serverless module isn't enabled. You'll need either cluster admin access to enable it, or the approach in this post.

Also verify the BTP Service Operator is available (needed for ServiceInstance and ServiceBinding):

kubectl api-resources | grep services.cloud.sap.com

You should see serviceinstances and servicebindings. If these are missing, stop here — the BTP Service Operator isn't installed and none of this will work.

Step 2: Create the Destination Service Instance and Binding

This is identical to the original post. Apply these to your namespace:

k8s/destination-instance.yaml

apiVersion: services.cloud.sap.com/v1
kind: ServiceInstance
metadata:
  name: destination-service
  namespace: dev-space-neil
spec:
  serviceOfferingName: destination
  servicePlanName: lite

k8s/destination-binding.yaml

apiVersion: services.cloud.sap.com/v1
kind: ServiceBinding
metadata:
  name: destination-service-binding
  namespace: dev-space-neil
spec:
  serviceInstanceName: destination-service
kubectl apply -f k8s/destination-instance.yaml
kubectl apply -f k8s/destination-binding.yaml

Wait until both show Ready: True:

kubectl get serviceinstance,servicebinding -n dev-space-neil

The binding creates a Kubernetes secret called destination-service-binding containing clientid, clientsecret, url (XSUAA token endpoint), and uri (Destination Service endpoint). Your container will read these as environment variables.

Step 3: Create the BTP Destination via REST API

If you have access to the BTP cockpit, create the destination there as described in the original post. If not, use the Destination Service REST API directly — the service binding credentials you just created have enough access to create instance-level destinations.

Get a token and POST the destination:

TOKEN=$(kubectl get secret destination-service-binding -n dev-space-neil \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.clientid}' | base64 -d)
SECRET=$(kubectl get secret destination-service-binding -n dev-space-neil \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.clientsecret}' | base64 -d)
XSUAA=$(kubectl get secret destination-service-binding -n dev-space-neil \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.url}' | base64 -d)
URI=$(kubectl get secret destination-service-binding -n dev-space-neil \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.uri}' | base64 -d)

ACCESS_TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST "$XSUAA/oauth/token" \
  -u "$TOKEN:$SECRET" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials" \
  | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['access_token'])")

curl -X POST "$URI/destination-configuration/v1/instanceDestinations" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "Name": "S4H_SANDBOX_BP",
    "Type": "HTTP",
    "URL": "https://sandbox.api.sap.com/s4hanacloud/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER",
    "Authentication": "NoAuthentication",
    "ProxyType": "Internet",
    "APIKey": "<your-api.sap.com-key>"
  }'

The distinction between subaccountDestinations and instanceDestinations matters here. Subaccount destinations are managed at the BTP subaccount level (requires admin access). Instance destinations are scoped to the service instance and can be created by the binding credentials — which is what we have.

Step 4: Write the Handler and HTTP Server

The business logic in handler.js is unchanged from the original post — get a token, resolve the destination, call the API. The only difference is gzip handling (the SAP API sandbox always compresses responses) and reading the API key from the destination properties rather than a separate Kubernetes secret.

handler.js

const https = require("https");
const http = require("http");
const zlib = require("zlib");

/**
 * Gets an OAuth2 access token from XSUAA using client credentials flow.
 * Credentials are injected as env vars from the destination-service-binding secret.
 */
async function getDestinationToken() {
  const credentials = Buffer.from(
    `${process.env.CLIENTID}:${process.env.CLIENTSECRET}`
  ).toString("base64");
  const tokenUrl = new URL(`${process.env.XSUAA_URL}/oauth/token`);

  return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    const options = {
      hostname: tokenUrl.hostname,
      path: `${tokenUrl.pathname}?grant_type=client_credentials`,
      method: "POST",
      headers: {
        Authorization: `Basic ${credentials}`,        // Base64-encoded clientid:clientsecret
        "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded",
      },
    };
    const req = https.request(options, (res) => {
      let data = "";
      res.on("data", (chunk) => (data += chunk));
      res.on("end", () => {
        try { resolve(JSON.parse(data).access_token); }
        catch (e) { reject(new Error(`Token parse failed: ${data}`)); }
      });
    });
    req.on("error", reject);
    req.end();
  });
}

/**
 * Resolves a named BTP Destination via the Destination Service REST API.
 * Returns the full destination object including destinationConfiguration,
 * which contains the target URL and any additional properties (e.g. APIKey).
 */
async function getDestination(name, token) {
  const destUrl = new URL(
    `${process.env.DESTINATION_URI}/destination-configuration/v1/destinations/${name}`
  );
  return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    const options = {
      hostname: destUrl.hostname,
      path: destUrl.pathname,
      method: "GET",
      headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}` },  // Token obtained from getDestinationToken()
    };
    const req = https.request(options, (res) => {
      let data = "";
      res.on("data", (chunk) => (data += chunk));
      res.on("end", () => {
        try { resolve(JSON.parse(data)); }
        catch (e) { reject(new Error(`Destination parse failed: ${data}`)); }
      });
    });
    req.on("error", reject);
    req.end();
  });
}

/**
 * Calls the SAP Business Partner API via the resolved destination.
 * The SAP API Hub sandbox always returns gzip-compressed responses,
 * so we explicitly handle gzip/deflate decompression via Node's zlib module.
 */
async function fetchBusinessPartners(destination) {
  const config = destination.destinationConfiguration;
  const targetUrl = new URL(`${config.URL}/A_BusinessPartner?$format=json&$top=20`);
  // Use http or https depending on the destination URL scheme
  const protocol = targetUrl.protocol === "https:" ? https : http;

  return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    const options = {
      hostname: targetUrl.hostname,
      path: `${targetUrl.pathname}${targetUrl.search}`,
      method: "GET",
      headers: {
        APIKey: config.APIKey,   // API key stored as a destination property, not a separate secret
        Accept: "application/json",
      },
    };
    const req = protocol.request(options, (res) => {
      const chunks = [];
      const encoding = res.headers["content-encoding"];
      // Pipe through the appropriate decompressor based on Content-Encoding header
      const stream =
        encoding === "gzip" ? res.pipe(zlib.createGunzip()) :
        encoding === "deflate" ? res.pipe(zlib.createInflate()) : res;
      stream.on("data", (chunk) => chunks.push(chunk));
      stream.on("end", () => {
        const raw = Buffer.concat(chunks).toString("utf8");
        try { resolve({ statusCode: res.statusCode, body: JSON.parse(raw) }); }
        catch (e) { resolve({ statusCode: res.statusCode, body: raw }); }
      });
      stream.on("error", reject);
    });
    req.on("error", reject);
    req.end();
  });
}

/**
 * Main entry point — orchestrates the full call chain:
 * 1. Get XSUAA token
 * 2. Resolve the S4H_SANDBOX_BP destination
 * 3. Fetch Business Partners from the SAP API sandbox
 * Returns a response object compatible with both Kyma Functions and the HTTP server wrapper.
 */
module.exports = {
  main: async function (event, context) {
    try {
      const token = await getDestinationToken();
      const destination = await getDestination("S4H_SANDBOX_BP", token);
      const result = await fetchBusinessPartners(destination);
      return {
        statusCode: result.statusCode,
        headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
        body: result.body,
      };
    } catch (err) {
      console.error("Error:", err.message);
      return {
        statusCode: 500,
        headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
        body: { error: err.message },
      };
    }
  },
};

Since this is a container rather than a Kyma Function, we need a simple HTTP server to drive it. server.js wraps the handler:

server.js

const http = require("http");
const handler = require("./handler");

const PORT = process.env.PORT || 8080;  // Default to 8080; override via env var if needed

/**
 * Minimal HTTP server that wraps handler.js for container deployment.
 * Kyma Functions have their own runtime that calls handler.main() directly —
 * this server provides the equivalent entry point when running as a standard Deployment.
 * Every request regardless of path/method triggers the Business Partner fetch.
 */
const server = http.createServer(async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const result = await handler.main({}, {});
    // handler.main() may return body as object or string — normalise to string
    const body = typeof result.body === "string"
      ? result.body
      : JSON.stringify(result.body);
    res.writeHead(result.statusCode || 200, {
      "Content-Type": "application/json",
      ...result.headers,
    });
    res.end(body);
  } catch (err) {
    res.writeHead(500, { "Content-Type": "application/json" });
    res.end(JSON.stringify({ error: err.message }));
  }
});

server.listen(PORT, () => console.log(`Listening on port ${PORT}`));

Step 5: Build and Push a Multi-Platform Container Image

This step doesn't exist in the original Kyma Function approach — the serverless runtime handles that for you. Here you need to build a Docker image and push it to a registry the cluster can reach.

Dockerfile

FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm install --omit=dev
COPY handler.js server.js ./
EXPOSE 8080
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

One important detail: if you're on an Apple Silicon Mac, Docker builds linux/arm64 by default. Most cloud Kubernetes clusters run linux/amd64. Build for both to avoid an ImagePullBackOff with the error no match for platform in manifest:

# Authenticate with GitHub Container Registry
echo $(gh auth token) | docker login ghcr.io -u <your-github-username> --password-stdin

# Build and push for both platforms
docker buildx build \
  --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 \
  -t ghcr.io/<your-github-username>/bp-function:latest \
  --push .

Make the package public in GitHub (your profile → Packages → bp-function → Package settings → Change visibility → Public) so the cluster can pull it without credentials.

Step 6: Deploy to Kyma

k8s/deployment.yaml

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: bp-function
  namespace: dev-space-neil
  labels:
    app: bp-function
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: bp-function
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: bp-function
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: bp-function
          image: ghcr.io/<your-github-username>/bp-function:latest
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          env:
            - name: CLIENTID
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: destination-service-binding
                  key: clientid
            - name: CLIENTSECRET
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: destination-service-binding
                  key: clientsecret
            - name: XSUAA_URL
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: destination-service-binding
                  key: url
            - name: DESTINATION_URI
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: destination-service-binding
                  key: uri
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 50m
              memory: 64Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 128Mi
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: bp-function
  namespace: dev-space-neil
spec:
  selector:
    app: bp-function
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8080

k8s/apirule.yaml

Use the full cluster domain rather than a short hostname. Find yours with:

kubectl get configmap shoot-info -n kube-system -o jsonpath='{.data.domain}'
apiVersion: gateway.kyma-project.io/v2
kind: APIRule
metadata:
  name: bp-function
  namespace: dev-space-neil
spec:
  hosts:
    - bp-function.<your-cluster-domain>.kyma.ondemand.com
  service:
    name: bp-function
    port: 80
  gateway: kyma-system/kyma-gateway
  rules:
    - path: /*
      methods:
        - GET
      noAuth: true

Apply both:

kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml
kubectl apply -f k8s/apirule.yaml

Check the pod is running:

kubectl get pods -n dev-space-neil -l app=bp-function

Step 7: Test

curl https://bp-function.<your-cluster-domain>.kyma.ondemand.com

You should see Business Partner records:

{
  "d": {
    "results": [
      {
        "BusinessPartner": "11",
        "BusinessPartnerFullName": "Cust15 Cust15",
        ...
      }
    ]
  }
}

How It Works

The call chain is the same as the original Kyma Function approach:

Internet -> APIRule (Istio) -> Service -> Pod
  -> XSUAA (get OAuth token)
  -> Destination Service (resolve S4H_SANDBOX_BP)
  -> SAP API Hub sandbox (fetch Business Partners)
  -> JSON response

The difference is that your code runs in a container you built and pushed, rather than in the Kyma serverless runtime. The BTP Service Operator still injects the Destination Service credentials into the pod as environment variables via the ServiceBinding secret — that part is identical.

Key Points

The Serverless module is optional. The BTP Service Operator, APIRule, and Istio are the parts that matter for this integration pattern. If Serverless isn't enabled, a standard Deployment works just as well.

Instance destinations vs subaccount destinations. If you can't create destinations in the BTP cockpit, use POST /destination-configuration/v1/instanceDestinations. These are scoped to the service instance and can be created using the binding credentials. They're resolved at runtime just like subaccount destinations.

Multi-platform builds matter. Apple Silicon Macs build linux/arm64 by default. Use docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 to produce a manifest list that works on both architectures — avoiding the cryptic no match for platform in manifest error.

Use the full APIRule hostname. In Kyma API Gateway v2, short hostnames in the hosts field expand to include the namespace (bp-function.dev-space-neil.<cluster-domain>). This may not match your cluster's wildcard DNS. Use the full explicit hostname (bp-function.<cluster-domain>) to be safe — check what format existing APIRules in your cluster use.

Gzip is not optional. The SAP API Business Hub sandbox always returns gzip-compressed responses regardless of what you put in Accept-Encoding. Handle it explicitly with Node's built-in zlib module.

entry.title: S/4HANA Public Cloud-Access Quotation Validity End Date & Attachment Changes in Workflow BAdI
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am working in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition and implementing the BAdI SD_APM_SET_APPROVAL_REASON for Sales Quotation approval scenarios.

My requirement is to set a custom approval reason when certain changes are made to an existing quotation. Specifically, I need to determine whether:

  1. The Quotation Validity End Date has been changed.

  2. A new attachment has been added to the quotation.

While debugging the BAdI, I can see the sales document header data being passed, but I cannot find the quotation validity end date in the available import structures. I can read the persisted value from the database using released APIs/CDS views, but I am unable to find the current value to compare against.

Similarly, I have not found any information in the BAdI context indicating whether attachments were added, removed, or modified.

My questions are:

  • Is there a released way in Public Cloud to access the current Quotation Validity End Date within BAdI SD_APM_SET_APPROVAL_REASON?

  • Is there any released API, CDS view, context object, or extension mechanism that exposes this field to the BAdI?

  • How can I determine whether an attachment was added to the quotation during the current change transaction?

  • Has anyone implemented approval logic based on quotation validity date changes or attachment changes in Public Cloud?

Any guidance or examples would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Onboarding Compliance Forms replication does not trigger for Rehires
entry.content:
Hi Community.

Sharing this, just as an FYI in case it saves someone the time in raising a support case.

The Onboarding Compliance Forms replication query, if configured, will replicate Onboarding data to ECP.

We observed that when someone was rehired and had previously gone through Onboarding, the Onboarding Compliance Forms replication query did not trigger.

SAP confirmed that this is expected behaviour but is something they are working on, possibly as earlier as H2 2026.

Recommended work around for now is to manually update ECP if there is updated compliance data for a rehire.

entry.title: Re: link failure on MSVC
entry.content:
Trying to answer my own question 😉

It looks like the samples building includes the additional files inside SDK\dbcapi directory which contains declaration/definition of API in question. I copied those 3 files but I now have compilation error. I did change the project settings to use /TP to compile as C++ code.

 

I am defining UNICODE and so compiling I get:

  1>c:\users\igor\onedrive\documents\dbhandler_app\libsqlany\sacapidll.c(57): error C2664: 'HMODULE LoadLibraryW(LPCWSTR)': cannot convert argument 1 from 'const char *' to 'LPCWSTR'

I am hesitant to change it, since it is not my code and I want to be on the safe side.

However I do believe that this needs to be fixed. Something like:

#ifdef _UNICODE

static void *loadLibrary(LPCWSTR name)

#else

static void *loadLiibrary(const char *name)

#endif

(Above is untested)

 

I'm sure someone on this forum can submit this as a bug/improvement.

 

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Onboarding - UK Compliance Form- Not triggering
entry.content: I'm not the original poster, but can confirm that we had a similar issue, and what Riife has recommended worked for us. We work mainly in NZ and Australia, and we encountered this issue for an NZ client, where state is not a field that is needed for the address config, so it felt counter-intuitive, but configuring it did resolve the issue, as it seems to be a system requirement for the back end jobs to run successfully

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud ALM - Health Monitoring
entry.content:
Hi Adani,

There is currently no in-built dashboard in Cloud ALM for displaying this type of Health Monitoring data.

With the SAP Cloud ALM Health Monitoring Analytics API, you can build dashboards and reports for health monitoring. It relies on the generic Analytics API for SAP Cloud ALM. More information can be found here: Health Monitoring Analytics API | SAP Help Portal

Regards,

Michael

SAP Support

entry.title: Joule Booster – "Setting Up Subaccount(s)" Step FAILED | SAP BTP - public cloud
entry.content:
Hello ,

I am trying to set up Joule using the SAP BTP Unified Booster for Joule provisioning, but the booster is failing during execution.

---

ISSUE SUMMARY

While running the Joule Booster in SAP BTP, the progress dialog shows:
• Setting up subaccount(s) → FAILED
• Setting up integrations → SKIPPED

Error message: "Setup encountered issues and could not be completed."

 

It created joule subscription but never created any formations/destinations. 

I'm trying to setup with Build work zone standard edition foundation plan.

entry.title: Re: Crystal Reports Runtime 13.0.35 / VB.NET issue with s...
entry.content:
I added Cr for VS Tag to your post.

First thing is update your runtime to the latest SP 40 from here:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/crystalreports/3354091173.html?locale=en-US&ai=true

Not sure other steps you are doing but have a look at my subreport logon code, it's C# but same steps:

//loop through all the sections to find all the subreport objects
foreach (CrystalDecisions.CrystalReports.Engine.Section crSection in crSections)
{
    crReportObjects = crSection.ReportObjects;
    //loop through all the report objects to find all the subreports
    foreach (CrystalDecisions.CrystalReports.Engine.ReportObject crReportObject in crReportObjects)
    {
        if (crReportObject.Kind == ReportObjectKind.SubreportObject)
        {
            try
            {
                ++flcnt;
                btnCount.Text = flcnt.ToString();

                //CrystalDecisions.CrystalReports.Engine.SubreportObject crSubreportObject;
                //CrystalDecisions.CrystalReports.Engine.ReportDocument crSubreportDocument;

                //you will need to typecast the reportobject to a subreport object once you find it
                crSubreportObject = (CrystalDecisions.CrystalReports.Engine.SubreportObject)crReportObject;
                string mysubname = crSubreportObject.SubreportName.ToString();

                //mysubname = " ";
                //open the subreport object
                crSubreportDocument = crSubreportObject.OpenSubreport(crSubreportObject.SubreportName);

                CrystalDecisions.CrystalReports.Engine.Database crSubDatabase;
                CrystalDecisions.CrystalReports.Engine.Tables crSubTables;
                crSubTables = crSubreportDocument.Database.Tables;
                TableLogOnInfo crTableSubLogOnInfo;

                CrystalDecisions.Shared.ConnectionInfo crSubConnectioninfo = new CrystalDecisions.Shared.ConnectionInfo();

                //set the database and tables objects to work with the subreport
                crSubDatabase = crSubreportDocument.Database;
                //crSubTables = crSubTables.Tables;
                tableIndex = 0;

                // get the DB name from the report
                CrystalDecisions.Shared.NameValuePair2 nvp2Sub = (NameValuePair2)crSubreportDocument.Database.Tables[tableIndex].LogOnInfo.ConnectionInfo.Attributes.Collection[1];

                // Log on and Get subreport SQl 
                SubreportController subreportController = rptClientDoc.SubreportController;
                SubreportClientDocument subreportClientDocument = subreportController.GetSubreport(crSubreportObject.SubreportName);

                // this gets the subreport linked fields so if more than one you will need to handle the values individually
                // since the main report parameter and record selection formula are only linked in the record selection formula you need to get the actual field the subreport is linked on to be able to get the SQL statement

                try
                {
                    SubreportLinks SubLinks = rptClientDoc.SubreportController.GetSubreportLinks(crSubreportDocument.Name.ToString());
                    for (int I = 0; I < SubLinks.Count; I++)
                    {
                        SubreportLink subLink = SubLinks[I];
                        string trimCurlies = subLink.LinkedParameterName.ToString();
                        char[] charsToTrim = { '{', '}', '?' }; // remove the {? ... } from the field name
                        trimCurlies = trimCurlies.Trim(charsToTrim);
                        subreportClientDocument.DataDefController.ParameterFieldController.SetCurrentValue(crSubreportObject.SubreportName.ToString(), trimCurlies.ToString(), "1"); // subreportLinkValue
                    }

                    foreach (CrystalDecisions.CrystalReports.Engine.Table crSubTable in crSubTables)
                    {
                        crSubConnectioninfo.UserID = btrFileLocation.Text.ToString();  
                        crSubConnectioninfo.Password = btrPassword.Text.ToString(); 
                        crSubConnectioninfo.ServerName = btrDataFile.Text.ToString();  
                        crConnectioninfo.DatabaseName = btrSearchPath.Text.ToString(); 
                     }
                        crTableSubLogOnInfo = crSubTable.LogOnInfo;
                        crTableSubLogOnInfo.ConnectionInfo = crSubConnectioninfo;

                        // this is where you set the main report parameter values
                        // if the subreport is not linked by the record selection formula it returns the first value, or last value depending on the subreport link
                        // so in this case where the subreport is linked via the record selection formula the value needs to be passed to the subreport Link.
                        //subreportLinkValue = "B8B1017103774ACF9878970D46016705";
                        //subreportLinkValue = "1003";
                        //SetCrystalParam(rpt, "@file_id", subreportLinkValue.ToString());

                        //SetCrystalParam(rpt, "nManualTemplateUID", "AC11CD27-6503-457C-93DF-1E9370E8E0F3");

                        //if (subreportClientDocument.EnableOnDemand == true)
                        //    subreportClientDocument.EnableOnDemand = false;
                        //IsRpt = false;

                        try
                        {
                            crSubTable.ApplyLogOnInfo(crTableSubLogOnInfo);
                        }
                        catch (Exception ex)
                        {
                            btnSQLStatement.Text += "ApplyLogOnInfo failed: " + ex.ToString();
                        }

                        //try
                        //{
                        //    if (crSubTable.TestConnectivity())
                        //        ConWorks = true;
                        //    else
                        //        ConWorks = false;
                        //}
                        //catch (Exception ex)
                        //{
                        //    btnSQLStatement.Text += "Test Connectivity failed: " + ex.ToString();
                        //}
                        //subreportClientDocument.DatabaseController.LogonEx(crConnectioninfo.ServerName, crConnectioninfo.DatabaseName, crConnectioninfo.UserID, crConnectioninfo.Password);

                        GroupPath gp1 = new GroupPath();
                        gp1.FromString("");
                        string sql = String.Empty;
                        subreportClientDocument.RowsetController.GetSQLStatement(gp1, out sql);

                        btnSQLStatement.Text += "\nSubreport: " + crSubreportObject.SubreportName.ToString() + "\n" + sql;
                        btnSQLStatement.AppendText("\n");
                        //break;
                    }
                }
                catch (Exception ex)
                {
                    {
                        if (nvp2Sub.Name.ToString() == crSubConnectioninfo.DatabaseName.ToString())
                        {
                            if (ex.Message == "Fetching SQL statements is not supported for this report.")
                            {
                                CrystalDecisions.ReportAppServer.Controllers.DatabaseController databaseController = rpt.ReportClientDocument.SubreportController.GetSubreport(crSubreportObject.SubreportName.ToString()).DatabaseController;
                                ISCRTable oldTable = (ISCRTable)databaseController.Database.Tables[0];
                                btnSQLStatement.Text += "\nSubreport: " + crSubreportObject.SubreportName.ToString() + " - Command:\n" + ((dynamic)oldTable).CommandText.ToString();
                                btnSQLStatement.AppendText("\n");
                            }
                            else
                            {
                                btnSQLStatement.Text += "Logon Subreport: " + crSubreportObject.SubreportName.ToString() + " - ERROR: " + ex.Message;
                                btnSQLStatement.AppendText("\n");
                            }
                        }
                        else
                            btnSQLStatement.Text += "\nWrong Subreport log on Server Name or Database: " + nvp2Sub.Value.ToString() + " does not exist or getting SQL from subreport not supported\n";
                    }
                }
            }
            catch (Exception ex)
            {
                {
                    if (ex.Message == "Fetching SQL statements is not supported for this report.")
                    {
                        btnSQLStatement.Text += "ERROR: " + ex.Message;
                        btnSQLStatement.AppendText("\n");
                    }
                    else
                    {
                        //btnSQLStatement.Text += "ERROR: " + ex.Message;
                        //btnSQLStatement.AppendText("\n");
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
if (btrVerifyDatabase.Checked)
{
    try
    {
        rpt.VerifyDatabase();
        // Verify required to Get the updated the Record Selection formula text
        // Record selection formula with comments included can only be retrieve via RAS
        myRecordSelectionWithComments = rptClientDoc.DataDefController.DataDefinition.RecordFilter;
        if (myRecordSelectionWithComments.FreeEditingText != null)
        {
            btnRecordSelectionForm.Text = "\n\n" + rpt.RecordSelectionFormula;
            btnRecordSelectionForm.AppendText("New Don\n\n");
        }
        else
            btnRecordSelectionForm.Text = "No Record Selection formula";
    }
    catch (Exception ex)
    {
        {
            btnSQLStatement.Text += "Failed to verify database with error: " + ex.Message;
            btnSQLStatement.AppendText("\n");
        }
    }
}
}
else
{
btnSQLStatement.Text += "No data source or failed to log on";
}

IsLoggedOn = true;
//try
//{
//    rpt.VerifyDatabase();
//}
//catch (Exception ex)
//{
//    MessageBox.Show("Trigger Event - Error in Viewer: " + e.ToString());
//}


//bool myTEst = rptClientDoc.DatabaseController.VerifyTableConnectivity("Orders");

// Pervasive
//rptClientDoc.DatabaseController.LogonEx("DEMODATA", "billing", "", ""); 

// update the SQL info
getReportOptionsOnOpen(rptClientDoc);
}

For a complete test app see these:

 

  • See these blogs on how to use Crystal Reports for Visual Studio sample applications:
    • How to use the new CR 2020 export to Microsoft Excel(XLXS) in Visual Studio .NET code (new)
    • How To: Parameters in Crystal Reports for Visual Studio .NET
    • Printing Crystal Reports in .NET
  • SAP Crystal Reports for Visual Studio Installation Guide ( SP 21 and above)

The parameter test app has the above code in it...

entry.title: Re: CR 2025 Mapping Fields
entry.content:
Crystal Reports 2025 is 64 bit application so you need a 64 bit Access Database client.

You can get it here:

https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/access/download-and-install-microsoft-365-access-runtime

So when selecting the OLE dB provider you should select this one, see attached oledb.jpg:

Now you should see the connection.

 

Possible Problem is connecting to the original source may be 32 bit and CR may have problems setting to a 64 bit source but see it that works.

entry.title: Re: Unable to expose RAP OData V4 UI app in Fiori Launchpad (not visible in Custom Catalog Extension
entry.content:
1) Yes you can expose RAP apps inside S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition using Launchpad Content Manager .

2) I don't think RAP app appear in custom catalog as RAP APP is part of developer extensibility.

3) Yes- Admin config will be needed

4) I am not sure on 4th question.

entry.title: Re: Custom App Interface Publishing Issues of ABAP Develo...
entry.content:
@junwu, I have sended IsActiveEntity=true in my payload for creation. But the fields can't be controlled as read-only on the frontend Fiori UI .

 

METHOD get_instance_features.
READ ENTITIES OF ZR_ZJKLOGTP IN LOCAL MODE
ENTITY zjklog
FIELDS ( overallstatus )
WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
RESULT DATA(new)
FAILED failed.
result = VALUE #( FOR newmt IN new
( %tky = newmt-%tky

%features-%delete "
= COND #( WHEN ( newmt-overallstatus IS INITIAL
)
* AND newmt-%is_draft = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
THEN if_abap_behv=>fc-o-enabled
ELSE if_abap_behv=>fc-o-disabled )

%action-edit "
= COND #( WHEN ( newmt-overallstatus IS INITIAL
* OR newmt-overallstatus = c_overall_status-new
)
"AND newmt-%is_draft = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
THEN if_abap_behv=>fc-o-enabled
ELSE if_abap_behv=>fc-o-disabled )
%field-sapobjecttype = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only
%field-sendsystem = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only
%field-senddatakey = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only
%field-sendkey = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only
%field-sendtype = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only
%field-sendbody = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only
%field-senddatetime = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only
%field-execstatus = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only
%field-exectimes = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only
%field-execresult = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only
%field-execbegindatetime = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only
%field-execenddatetime = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only
%field-sapdatakey1 = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only
%field-sapdatakey2 = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only
%field-sapdatakey3 = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only


) ).
ENDMETHOD.
